content.problemStatement:
The functional array of an array A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \dots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​] is the array fAfAfA of size N−1N-1N−1, where fAi=Ai+1−AifA_i = A_{i+1} - A_ifAi​=Ai+1​−Ai​ for 1≤i<N1\leq i \lt N1≤i<N. For example, if A=[2,3,9,11]A = [2, 3, 9, 11]A=[2,3,9,11] then fA=[1,6,2]fA = [1, 6, 2]fA=[1,6,2].

You are given two arrays B=[B1,B2,…,BN]B = [B_1, B_2, \dots, B_N]B=[B1​,B2​,…,BN​] and Z=[Z1,Z2,…,ZM]Z = [Z_1, Z_2, \dots, Z_M]Z=[Z1​,Z2​,…,ZM​] of length NNN and MMM respectively. Find out whether there exists an array AAA such that:


BBB is a subsequence of AAA, and
fAfAfA is a subsequence of ZZZ
Print "YES" (without quotes) if such an array AAA exists, and "NO" otherwise.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case contains three lines of input.
The first line contains two integers NNN and MMM - the lengths of BBB and ZZZ respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \dots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​; the elements of BBB.
The third line contains MMM space-separated integers Z1,Z2,…,ZMZ_1, Z_2, \dots, Z_MZ1​,Z2​,…,ZM​; the elements of ZZZ.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing one word - either "YES" or "NO". You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes" and "YES" will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
Sum of M over all test cases ≤ 10^5
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of three lines:
- The first line contains two integers N and M – the lengths of arrays B and Z respectively.
- The second line contains N space‑separated integers B1 … BN.
- The third line contains M space‑separated integers Z1 … ZM.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if there exists an array A satisfying the conditions, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1:
A=[1,2,3]A = [1, 2, 3]A=[1,2,3] is one possible array.

B=[2,3]B = [2, 3]B=[2,3] is a subsequence of AAA
fA=[1,1]fA = [1, 1]fA=[1,1] is a subsequence of Z=[1,1]Z = [1, 1]Z=[1,1].

Test case 2:
It can be shown that no array AAA can both contain B=[2,4]B = [2, 4]B=[2,4] as a subsequence and also have fAfAfA be a subsequence of Z=[1,3]Z = [1, 3]Z=[1,3]

solution.approach:
The functional array fA consists of consecutive differences A[i+1]‑A[i]. Because every element of Z is non‑negative, any element of fA (which must be a subsequence of Z) is also non‑negative. Hence A must be a non‑decreasing array and the given subsequence B must itself be non‑decreasing; otherwise the answer is immediately NO.

If B is non‑decreasing, let delta_k = B_{k+1}‑B_k (k = 1 … N‑1). We have to choose, from Z while preserving order, a (possibly empty) group of numbers whose sum equals each delta_k. The groups must appear consecutively – after finishing one group we start the next. Because all Z‑values are ≥0, a greedy scan works: iterate through Z, accumulate a running sum cur for the current delta. When cur equals delta_k we move to the next delta and reset cur to 0. If cur ever exceeds delta_k, the required sum cannot be formed (we cannot subtract numbers), so the answer is NO. A delta equal to 0 needs no numbers, i.e., an empty group, and we simply continue.

If all deltas are satisfied, any remaining elements of Z can be ignored, and we can always prepend or append arbitrary numbers to A (they do not affect the subsequence B). For N = 1 there are no deltas, so the answer is always YES.

The algorithm runs in O(N + M) per test case using two pointers and 64‑bit integers for sums.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M) per test case, where N and M are the lengths of B and Z.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra besides the input arrays.
